topics.title: AWS Lambda — Serverless Compute
topics.description: Lambda lets you run code without provisioning or managing servers. You upload a function, define its trigger, and AWS handles everything else — scaling from 0 to 10,000 concurrent executions automatically, billing only for actual compute time used.
topics.story: Imagine a vending machine vs. a restaurant kitchen. EC2 is a restaurant kitchen — always on, staff always present, costs money even when no orders come in. Lambda is a vending machine — no kitchen, no staff, but the moment you press a button (an event), it instantly dispenses the item and goes back to doing nothing. You only pay for the milliseconds the machine is working.
topics.analogy: Lambda function = a recipe card that comes to life only when called. Event = someone handing you the ingredients. Runtime = the kitchen Lambda provisions in milliseconds. Execution context = the temporary workspace that exists while you cook. Cold start = the first time you cook today (slightly slower, kitchen is cold). Warm start = cooking again immediately after — kitchen is still hot. 15-minute timeout = maximum cooking time before Lambda shuts you down.
topics.memoryAnchor: Lambda = 'λ' function in math — takes an input, returns an output, stateless. The billing unit is 'GB-seconds': (memory in GB) × (duration in seconds). Max timeout = 15 minutes. Max memory = 10 GB. Concurrent executions = number of functions running simultaneously (default limit: 1,000 per region).
topics.firstPrinciples: Lambda is built on microVM technology (Firecracker, open-sourced by AWS) — each invocation runs in an isolated lightweight VM that boots in ~125ms. The execution model is: event arrives → Lambda service routes to a warm worker or boots a new one (cold start) → function code executes → response returned → worker is frozen (not terminated) for reuse. State does NOT persist between invocations (the /tmp directory is an exception — 512MB-10GB ephemeral storage, may or may not survive). The invocation model: synchronous (API Gateway, wait for response), asynchronous (S3 events, SNS — Lambda retries on failure), event source mapping (SQS/Kinesis — Lambda polls the queue).
topics.bruteForce: Without Lambda: run a small EC2 t3.nano 24/7 to handle occasional events — $0.0052/hour × 8760 hours = ~$45/year even when idle. With Lambda: 1 million events/month × 100ms each × 128MB memory = ~$0.20/month. Lambda is 200× cheaper for sporadic workloads.
topics.optimizedApproach: Minimize cold starts: use Provisioned Concurrency for latency-sensitive functions (pre-warmed instances), keep deployment packages small (tree-shaking, Layer sharing), prefer Node.js/Python for faster cold starts over Java (JVM startup). Optimize memory: Lambda CPU is proportional to memory — a 1024MB function gets 2× CPU of 512MB but costs 2×. Benchmark to find optimal memory. Reuse connections: initialise database clients and SDK clients outside the handler function (in the global scope of the module) so they survive across warm invocations — connection setup is amortized across many requests.
topics.whenToUse: Lambda: event-driven processing (S3 uploads, DynamoDB streams, SQS messages), APIs with variable traffic (API Gateway + Lambda), scheduled tasks (cron via EventBridge), data transformation pipelines, webhooks. NOT Lambda: long-running jobs (>15 min), WebSocket-heavy workloads requiring persistent connections, workloads requiring >10GB memory or GPU, latency-critical paths where cold starts are unacceptable (use Provisioned Concurrency or EC2).

examples.title: Lambda Function — Handler, Context, and Event
examples.code:
import json
import boto3
import os
from datetime import datetime

# Module-level initialisation — runs ONCE per container lifecycle
# (reused across warm invocations — saves ~50ms per call)
dynamodb = boto3.resource('dynamodb')
table = dynamodb.Table(os.environ['TABLE_NAME'])

def handler(event, context):
    """
    Lambda handler signature: event (dict) + context (runtime info object)
    
    context provides:
      context.function_name       → 'my-api-function'
      context.memory_limit_in_mb  → 128
      context.get_remaining_time_in_millis() → ms until timeout
      context.aws_request_id      → unique invocation ID for tracing
    """
    print(f"Request ID: {context.aws_request_id}")
    print(f"Remaining time: {context.get_remaining_time_in_millis()}ms")
    
    # event shape depends on the trigger
    # API Gateway event example:
    http_method = event.get('httpMethod', 'UNKNOWN')
    path = event.get('path', '/')
    body = json.loads(event.get('body') or '{}')
    
    user_id = body.get('userId')
    if not user_id:
        return {
            'statusCode': 400,
            'headers': {'Content-Type': 'application/json'},
            'body': json.dumps({'error': 'userId is required'})
        }
    
    # Business logic
    item = {
        'userId': user_id,
        'timestamp': datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        'action': body.get('action', 'unknown')
    }
    table.put_item(Item=item)
    
    return {
        'statusCode': 201,
        'headers': {
            'Content-Type': 'application/json',
            'Access-Control-Allow-Origin': '*'
        },
        'body': json.dumps({'message': 'Created', 'userId': user_id})
    }
examples.explanation: The handler receives two arguments: event (the trigger payload — shape varies by trigger source) and context (runtime metadata). Module-level code runs during container initialisation and is reused across warm invocations — put SDK client init, DB connection, and config loading here. The return value for API Gateway triggers must be a dict with statusCode, headers, and body (as a JSON string). Lambda automatically serialises logs to CloudWatch Logs — all print() / logging output lands in the function's log group.
examples.pseudocode:
Lambda invocation lifecycle:
1. Event arrives (API Gateway, S3, SQS, etc.)
2. Lambda service checks warm pool:
   a. Warm worker available? → send event to existing container
   b. No warm worker? → COLD START:
      - Boot Firecracker microVM (~50ms)
      - Download deployment package / container image
      - Start runtime (Python/Node/Java interpreter)
      - Run module-level code (import statements, client init)
      Total cold start: 100ms – 1000ms depending on runtime + package size
3. Call handler(event, context)
4. Handler returns response
5. Container frozen (NOT terminated) for reuse
6. Billing: ceil(duration_ms / 1ms) × (memory_gb) × $0.0000166667
examples.realWorldUse: Image thumbnail generation on S3 upload (Lambda triggered by S3 PutObject event). Email notifications on database changes (Lambda triggered by DynamoDB stream). REST API endpoints for mobile apps via API Gateway. Scheduled data processing (EventBridge cron triggers Lambda every hour). Slack bot webhook handler (API Gateway → Lambda → Slack API).
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  E[Event Source] -->|invoke| LS[Lambda Service]
  LS --> WP{Warm pool?}
  WP -->|Yes| WI[Warm invocation]
  WP -->|No| CS[Cold Start]
  CS --> Boot[Boot microVM]
  Boot --> Import[Run module-level code]
  Import --> WI
  WI --> H[handler event context]
  H --> Resp[Return response]
  Resp --> Freeze[Freeze container]
  Freeze -->|next invocation| WP
  H --> CW[CloudWatch Logs]

examples.title: Cold Starts, Layers, and Provisioned Concurrency
examples.code:
# ── Lambda Layers — shared dependencies across functions ──────────────
# Create a layer with heavy dependencies (numpy, pandas)
mkdir -p python/lib/python3.12/site-packages
pip install numpy pandas -t python/lib/python3.12/site-packages
zip -r data-science-layer.zip python/

aws lambda publish-layer-version \
  --layer-name data-science-deps \
  --zip-file fileb://data-science-layer.zip \
  --compatible-runtimes python3.12

# Attach layer to a function (up to 5 layers per function, total 250MB unzipped)
aws lambda update-function-configuration \
  --function-name my-ml-function \
  --layers arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789:layer:data-science-deps:1

# ── Provisioned Concurrency — eliminate cold starts ────────────────────
# Pre-warm 10 instances so they're always ready
aws lambda put-provisioned-concurrency-config \
  --function-name my-api-function \
  --qualifier prod \
  --provisioned-concurrent-executions 10
# Cost: ~$0.015 per GB-hour (on top of regular invocation cost)
# Benefit: p99 latency drops from 800ms to 10ms for cold-start-heavy functions

# ── Environment variables and secrets ──────────────────────────────────
aws lambda update-function-configuration \
  --function-name my-api-function \
  --environment Variables={\
    TABLE_NAME=user-events,\
    REGION=us-east-1,\
    LOG_LEVEL=INFO\
  }

# ── Concurrency limits — protect downstream services ──────────────────
# Reserve 100 concurrent executions for a critical function
# (prevents other functions from stealing all 1000 account-level concurrency)
aws lambda put-function-concurrency \
  --function-name critical-payment-function \
  --reserved-concurrent-executions 100

# Set throttle to 0 (temporarily disable a function)
aws lambda put-function-concurrency \
  --function-name non-critical-function \
  --reserved-concurrent-executions 0

# ── Deploy via ZIP ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
zip function.zip handler.py
aws lambda update-function-code \
  --function-name my-api-function \
  --zip-file fileb://function.zip
examples.explanation: Lambda Layers solve the 'shared dependencies' problem — package common libraries (numpy, boto3 extensions, internal utilities) as a layer and attach it to multiple functions, reducing deployment package sizes and speeding up cold starts. The function's own ZIP should contain only your business logic code. Provisioned Concurrency keeps a specified number of function instances pre-initialised and warm — the first request never waits for a cold start. Reserved Concurrency guarantees a function always has concurrency available (protected from noisy neighbours) but also caps its max concurrency (circuit breaker pattern for downstream protection).
examples.pseudocode:
Cold start cost breakdown for Java Lambda (worst case):
  JVM startup:          ~400ms
  Class loading:        ~200ms
  Spring context init:  ~500ms (Spring Boot Lambda)
  Module-level code:    ~50ms
  Total cold start:     ~1150ms

With Provisioned Concurrency:
  All initialisation done at provision time
  Handler invocation:   ~5ms
  Total cold start:     0ms (always warm)

Layer lookup order at invocation:
  1. Function code (unzipped to /var/task/)
  2. Layer 1 (unzipped to /opt/)
  3. Layer 2 (unzipped to /opt/) — later layers override earlier ones
  Note: /opt/python/ is added to PYTHONPATH automatically for Python runtimes
examples.realWorldUse: E-commerce checkout Lambda: use Provisioned Concurrency at 20 instances during business hours (scaled down via scheduled EventBridge rule at night). Data science functions: numpy + pandas + scikit-learn in a shared Layer (300MB) attached to 10 different ML functions — only stored once. Payment processing function with reserved concurrency of 50: protects the payment database from being overwhelmed if other functions spike.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  Deploy[Deploy function code ZIP] --> FC[Function Container]
  L1[Layer 1: numpy+pandas] --> FC
  L2[Layer 2: internal-utils] --> FC
  FC --> PC[Provisioned Concurrency: 10 warm instances]
  PC --> Inv1[Invocation 1: 5ms]
  PC --> Inv2[Invocation 2: 5ms]
  PC --> Inv3[Invocation 3: 5ms]
  FC --> OD[On-demand pool]
  OD --> CS[Cold start: 1000ms]
  RC[Reserved Concurrency: 100] -->|cap| FC

examples.title: Lambda Triggers — S3, EventBridge, and SQS
examples.code:
import json
import boto3
import urllib.parse
from PIL import Image
import io

s3 = boto3.client('s3')

# ── Trigger 1: S3 Event (image upload → thumbnail generation) ──────────
def s3_handler(event, context):
    """Triggered when object is PUT to S3 bucket."""
    for record in event['Records']:
        bucket = record['s3']['bucket']['name']
        key = urllib.parse.unquote_plus(record['s3']['object']['key'])
        
        # Download original image
        response = s3.get_object(Bucket=bucket, Key=key)
        original = Image.open(io.BytesIO(response['Body'].read()))
        
        # Create 200x200 thumbnail
        thumbnail = original.copy()
        thumbnail.thumbnail((200, 200))
        
        # Upload thumbnail to a different prefix
        buffer = io.BytesIO()
        thumbnail.save(buffer, format='JPEG', quality=85)
        buffer.seek(0)
        
        thumb_key = key.replace('uploads/', 'thumbnails/')
        s3.put_object(Bucket=bucket, Key=thumb_key, Body=buffer.getvalue())
        print(f"Thumbnail created: {thumb_key}")

# ── Trigger 2: EventBridge Scheduled Rule (daily report) ───────────────
def scheduled_handler(event, context):
    """
    Triggered by EventBridge cron: cron(0 9 * * ? *) = 9am UTC every day
    event = {
      'version': '0',
      'id': 'some-uuid',
      'source': 'aws.events',
      'detail-type': 'Scheduled Event',
      'time': '2026-04-10T09:00:00Z'
    }
    """
    print(f"Running daily report for {event['time']}")
    # ... generate report, send email via SES ...
    return {'status': 'report_sent'}

# ── Trigger 3: SQS Event (batch message processing) ────────────────────
def sqs_handler(event, context):
    """
    SQS trigger: Lambda polls the queue, delivers up to batchSize messages.
    event['Records'] contains 1-10 messages (configurable batch size).
    To fail individual messages (partial batch failure), raise exception
    only for messages that failed, or use ReportBatchItemFailures.
    """
    failed_ids = []
    
    for record in event['Records']:
        message_id = record['messageId']
        receipt_handle = record['receiptHandle']
        body = json.loads(record['body'])
        
        try:
            process_order(body)
            # Lambda auto-deletes from SQS only if handler succeeds
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Failed to process {message_id}: {e}")
            failed_ids.append({'itemIdentifier': message_id})
    
    # Return failed message IDs for partial batch failure
    # These stay in queue for retry; successful ones are auto-deleted
    return {'batchItemFailures': [{'itemIdentifier': mid} for mid in failed_ids]}

def process_order(order):
    print(f"Processing order {order['orderId']} for user {order['userId']}")
examples.explanation: Lambda supports 20+ native trigger sources. S3 triggers are asynchronous — Lambda receives the event after S3 stores the object, processes it, and the caller doesn't wait. EventBridge (formerly CloudWatch Events) triggers are the AWS cron job equivalent — define a cron or rate expression and Lambda runs on schedule. SQS triggers use the event source mapping model — Lambda's internal poller calls ReceiveMessage on the queue every few seconds, batches messages (1-10000), and invokes your function. If the function succeeds, Lambda automatically calls DeleteMessage. If it fails, messages return to the queue (or go to the Dead Letter Queue after max retries).
examples.pseudocode:
SQS trigger event source mapping internals:
  Lambda service polls SQS:
    ReceiveMessage(QueueUrl, MaxNumberOfMessages=batchSize, WaitTimeSeconds=20)
  If messages returned:
    Invoke function with batch as event['Records']
    If function returns 200 (success):
      DeleteMessage for ALL messages in batch
    If function throws exception:
      Do NOT delete → messages become visible again after VisibilityTimeout
      After maxReceiveCount retries → move to DLQ
  
  Partial batch failure (batchItemFailures response):
    Delete only messages NOT in batchItemFailures
    Retry only failed messages
examples.realWorldUse: S3 trigger: auto-generate thumbnails, extract text from PDFs (Textract), transcode videos (MediaConvert). EventBridge schedule: daily database cleanup, weekly report generation, hourly cache invalidation. SQS trigger: async order processing, email notification queue, event sourcing pipeline. SNS trigger: fan-out notifications (SNS → multiple SQS queues → multiple Lambda functions for parallel processing).
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  S3[S3 Bucket upload] -->|PutObject event| L1[Lambda: s3_handler]
  L1 --> Thumb[Generate thumbnail]
  Thumb --> S3t[S3 thumbnails/]
  EB[EventBridge: cron 9am] -->|Scheduled Event| L2[Lambda: scheduled_handler]
  L2 --> Report[Generate + email report]
  SQS[SQS Order Queue] -->|batch poll| L3[Lambda: sqs_handler]
  L3 --> PO[process_order]
  PO -->|success| Del[Auto-delete from SQS]
  PO -->|failure| Vis[Return to queue]
  Vis -->|5 retries| DLQ[Dead Letter Queue]

problems.title: Design a Lambda Function for Thumbnail Generation
problems.description: Write the pseudo-code / logic for a Lambda function triggered by S3 ObjectCreated events. It should: (1) Extract the bucket name and object key from the event, (2) Skip processing if the key already starts with 'thumbnails/' to prevent infinite loops, (3) Download the image, resize to 150x150, (4) Upload to the same bucket under 'thumbnails/' prefix.
constraints: event['Records'] is a list — process all records
URL-decode the object key (spaces become '+' in S3 event keys)
Source bucket and destination bucket can be the same if prefix differs

examples.output: Thumbnail saved to 'thumbnails/user/photo.jpg' in same bucket
examples.explanation: The guard clause (skip if key starts with 'thumbnails/') prevents the thumbnail upload from triggering another Lambda invocation

hints: urllib.parse.unquote_plus() decodes the key
Check key.startswith('thumbnails/') and return early
Use Pillow Image.thumbnail() — it maintains aspect ratio unlike resize()
problems.sampleOutput: Thumbnail saved to 'thumbnails/user/photo.jpg' in same bucket

problems.title: Lambda Cold Start Optimisation
problems.description: A Java Lambda function serving a REST API has a p99 latency of 1800ms and p50 of 40ms. The function uses Spring Boot and initialises a DynamoDB client on each invocation. Identify 3 specific problems with this setup and propose concrete fixes for each.
constraints: The function handles 500 requests/day unevenly distributed
Peak traffic is 50 req/min for 2 hours, near-zero otherwise
Budget allows ~$50/month extra for latency improvements

examples.input: p99=1800ms, p50=40ms gap indicates cold starts
examples.output: Fix 1: Move DynamoDB client to module scope. Fix 2: Provisioned Concurrency=5 during peak hours via EventBridge schedule. Fix 3: Use AWS Lambda SnapStart (Java only) to reduce JVM cold start from ~1s to ~100ms
examples.explanation: The p99/p50 gap is the telltale cold start signature — most requests are fast (warm), occasional ones are very slow (cold)

hints: Module-scope initialisation runs once per container lifecycle
SnapStart takes a snapshot after init phase — restores from snapshot instead of reinitialising
Scheduled Provisioned Concurrency scale-up/down with EventBridge saves cost vs always-on
problems.sampleInput: p99=1800ms, p50=40ms gap indicates cold starts
problems.sampleOutput: Fix 1: Move DynamoDB client to module scope. Fix 2: Provisioned Concurrency=5 during peak hours via EventBridge schedule. Fix 3: Use AWS Lambda SnapStart (Java only) to reduce JVM cold start from ~1s to ~100ms

problems.title: SQS Dead Letter Queue Setup
problems.description: An order processing Lambda fails occasionally due to external payment API timeouts. Messages that fail 3 times should be moved to a Dead Letter Queue for manual review. Design the SQS queue configuration (maxReceiveCount, visibility timeout) and explain how partial batch failures work when Lambda processes 5 messages and 2 fail.
constraints: Payment API calls take up to 8 seconds
Lambda batch size = 5 messages
Visibility timeout must be greater than Lambda timeout

examples.input: Lambda timeout=30s, batch size=5, 2 messages fail processing
examples.output: VisibilityTimeout=35s (> Lambda timeout), maxReceiveCount=3, partial batch: return batchItemFailures with 2 failed messageIds
examples.explanation: If visibility timeout < Lambda timeout, messages become visible mid-processing causing duplicate processing

hints: Visibility timeout should be at least 6× the Lambda timeout for safety
batchItemFailures response tells Lambda which messages to NOT delete
DLQ is just another SQS queue — set it as the redrive policy target
problems.sampleInput: Lambda timeout=30s, batch size=5, 2 messages fail processing
problems.sampleOutput: VisibilityTimeout=35s (> Lambda timeout), maxReceiveCount=3, partial batch: return batchItemFailures with 2 failed messageIds

problems.title: Lambda Execution Role Permissions
problems.description: Write the IAM permission policy for a Lambda function that needs to: read messages from an SQS queue, write items to a DynamoDB table named 'orders', write logs to CloudWatch Logs, and read a secret from Secrets Manager named '/prod/payment-api-key'.
constraints: Least-privilege: only grant exactly what's needed
CloudWatch Logs requires 3 permissions: CreateLogGroup, CreateLogStream, PutLogEvents
SQS consumer needs: ReceiveMessage, DeleteMessage, GetQueueAttributes

examples.input: Lambda processes orders: reads SQS, writes DynamoDB, logs, reads secret
examples.output: 4-statement policy: sqs:ReceiveMessage+DeleteMessage+GetQueueAttributes on queue ARN | dynamodb:PutItem+GetItem on table ARN | logs:CreateLogGroup+CreateLogStream+PutLogEvents | secretsmanager:GetSecretValue on secret ARN
examples.explanation: Each statement targets a specific resource ARN — never use * as Resource in production Lambda policies

hints: DynamoDB table ARN: arn:aws:dynamodb:region:account:table/orders
Secrets Manager ARN: arn:aws:secretsmanager:region:account:secret:/prod/payment-api-key-*
Lambda auto-creates the CloudWatch Log group if CreateLogGroup permission is present

topics.title: Amazon API Gateway — HTTP APIs and REST APIs
topics.description: API Gateway is a fully managed service for creating, publishing, and securing APIs at any scale. It acts as the front door for applications, routing HTTP requests to Lambda, EC2, or any HTTP endpoint — handling auth, throttling, caching, and request/response transformation.
topics.story: Think of API Gateway as a high-end hotel concierge desk. Guests (API clients) don't walk directly into the kitchen (Lambda) or the laundry room (backend services). They go through the concierge (API Gateway) who validates their key card (auth), routes them to the right department, enforces rules (rate limits), and returns a polished response. The concierge also handles the translation — guests speak English (JSON/HTTP) but the kitchen staff might need the order in a specific internal format.
topics.analogy: API Gateway = the NGINX/reverse proxy of the cloud, but fully managed. Route = URL pattern + HTTP method. Integration = where to forward the request (Lambda, HTTP URL, AWS service). Stage = deployment environment (dev, staging, prod) — each has its own URL. Throttling = 10,000 RPS per account by default, configurable per route. Authoriser = a Lambda or Cognito that gates access before your backend sees the request.
topics.memoryAnchor: Two types: REST API (V1, more features, higher cost ~$3.50/million) and HTTP API (V2, simpler, cheaper ~$1/million, lower latency). WebSocket API for bidirectional communication. Default throttling: 10,000 requests/second burst, 5,000 steady-state per region. Payload size limit: 10MB. Timeout: maximum 29 seconds (Lambda's 15-minute limit does NOT apply here — API Gateway has its own 29s timeout).
topics.firstPrinciples: API Gateway receives an HTTP request, constructs an event payload (the JSON object passed to Lambda), waits for the response, and translates it back to an HTTP response. For Lambda proxy integration (the default and recommended approach): API Gateway passes the raw request as-is to Lambda, and Lambda returns a structured response object ({statusCode, headers, body}). API Gateway trusts Lambda's response completely. For non-proxy (mapping templates): API Gateway transforms request/response using VTL (Velocity Template Language) — powerful but complex, rarely needed with modern Lambda. HTTP API (V2) is the modern choice — 60% cheaper, supports JWT authorisers natively, automatic CORS, but lacks some REST API features (usage plans, API keys, request/response transforms, WAF integration).
topics.bruteForce: Without API Gateway: configure an ALB to route to Lambda — works but lacks built-in auth, throttling, API keys, usage plans, request validation, stage management, and detailed per-route metrics. Cost is similar but operationally more complex.
topics.optimizedApproach: HTTP API for new projects (V2): simpler, cheaper, faster. Use Lambda proxy integration always — avoid VTL mapping templates (unmaintainable). Enable caching on GET endpoints with stable data (reduces Lambda invocations, lowers cost). Use Lambda authorisers for custom auth logic or Cognito JWT authorisers for standard JWT validation. Enable WAF (Web Application Firewall) integration for internet-facing APIs. Use Usage Plans + API Keys for B2B APIs to track and limit partner consumption.
topics.whenToUse: API Gateway: any serverless REST or HTTP API, mobile/SPA backends, microservice APIs, webhook receivers. NOT API Gateway: WebRTC or persistent WebSocket (API Gateway WebSocket has 10-minute idle timeout), file uploads > 10MB (use S3 presigned URLs instead), APIs requiring < 10ms p99 latency (ALB + EC2/ECS has lower overhead).

examples.title: HTTP API — Routes, Lambda Integration, and CORS
examples.code:
# AWS SAM (Serverless Application Model) template
# Defines an HTTP API Gateway + Lambda functions
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31

Globals:
  Function:
    Runtime: python3.12
    MemorySize: 256
    Timeout: 10
    Environment:
      Variables:
        TABLE_NAME: !Ref UsersTable

Resources:
  # HTTP API (V2) — cheaper and simpler than REST API
  MyHttpApi:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::HttpApi
    Properties:
      StageName: prod
      CorsConfiguration:
        AllowOrigins:
          - 'https://myapp.com'
          - 'http://localhost:3000'
        AllowMethods:
          - GET
          - POST
          - DELETE
        AllowHeaders:
          - Authorization
          - Content-Type
        MaxAge: 300  # Browser caches preflight for 5 minutes

  # Lambda function — handles GET /users and POST /users
  UsersFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Handler: users.handler
      Policies:
        - DynamoDBCrudPolicy:
            TableName: !Ref UsersTable
      Events:
        GetUsers:
          Type: HttpApi
          Properties:
            ApiId: !Ref MyHttpApi
            Method: GET
            Path: /users
        CreateUser:
          Type: HttpApi
          Properties:
            ApiId: !Ref MyHttpApi
            Method: POST
            Path: /users
        GetUser:
          Type: HttpApi
          Properties:
            ApiId: !Ref MyHttpApi
            Method: GET
            Path: /users/{userId}  # Path parameter

  UsersTable:
    Type: AWS::DynamoDB::Table
    Properties:
      BillingMode: PAY_PER_REQUEST
      AttributeDefinitions:
        - AttributeName: userId
          AttributeType: S
      KeySchema:
        - AttributeName: userId
          KeyType: HASH

Outputs:
  ApiEndpoint:
    Value: !Sub 'https://${MyHttpApi}.execute-api.${AWS::Region}.amazonaws.com/prod'
examples.explanation: SAM (Serverless Application Model) is CloudFormation with Lambda-aware shorthand. HTTP API V2 is defined once, and multiple Lambda functions register routes on it. CORS is configured at the API level — API Gateway handles preflight OPTIONS requests automatically. Path parameters ({userId}) are passed in event['pathParameters']['userId']. The DynamoDBCrudPolicy SAM policy macro generates the correct DynamoDB IAM permissions automatically. The API URL format is https://{api-id}.execute-api.{region}.amazonaws.com/{stage}.
examples.pseudocode:
API Gateway HTTP request flow:
  Client: GET https://abc123.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/prod/users/u42
  
  API Gateway:
    1. Match route: GET /users/{userId} → UsersFunction Lambda
    2. Check authoriser (if configured) — skip if none
    3. Build Lambda event:
       {
         version: '2.0',
         routeKey: 'GET /users/{userId}',
         pathParameters: {userId: 'u42'},
         queryStringParameters: {},
         headers: {authorization: 'Bearer eyJ...'},
         requestContext: {accountId: '...', stage: 'prod'}
       }
    4. Invoke Lambda synchronously
    5. Lambda returns: {statusCode: 200, headers: {...}, body: '{...}'}
    6. API Gateway forwards statusCode + headers + body to client
    7. If Lambda returns error / times out (>29s): 502 Bad Gateway
examples.realWorldUse: React SPA on CloudFront calling GET /api/products, POST /api/cart from API Gateway → Lambda → DynamoDB. Mobile app backend: HTTP API V2 with Cognito JWT authoriser — no server management, scales to millions of users. Webhook receiver: POST /webhook → Lambda validates signature → processes event → returns 200 immediately (async processing via SQS).

examples.title: Lambda Authorisers and Usage Plans
examples.explanation: Lambda authorisers decouple authentication from business logic. The authoriser receives the raw request (or just the token in TOKEN type) and returns an IAM policy. API Gateway caches the policy by principal ID for a configurable TTL (default 300s) — the authoriser Lambda is NOT invoked on every request during the cache window. The context dict flows through to the backend Lambda via event['requestContext']['authorizer'], enabling the backend to use auth results (user ID, role, plan tier) without re-parsing the token. Usage Plans + API Keys (REST API only) provide metered access — define a plan with 1000 requests/day quota and 10 RPS throttle, issue API keys to partners, and API Gateway enforces the limits automatically.
examples.pseudocode:
Authoriser cache behaviour:
  Request 1: GET /data with token=abc123
    → Authoriser Lambda invoked
    → Returns ALLOW policy for principalId='user-42'
    → API Gateway caches: {token: 'abc123', policy: ALLOW, context: {...}} for 300s
    → Backend Lambda invoked
  
  Request 2 (within 300s): GET /data with token=abc123
    → Cache HIT: skip authoriser Lambda invocation
    → Backend Lambda invoked directly
    → Saves: ~10ms authoriser latency + Lambda cost
  
  Request 3: GET /data with token=INVALID
    → Authoriser Lambda invoked
    → Raises 'Unauthorized'
    → API Gateway returns 401, does NOT invoke backend Lambda
examples.realWorldUse: SaaS product: Lambda authoriser validates JWT + looks up subscription tier in DynamoDB, returns context with plan=pro/free/enterprise. Backend functions use plan tier to gate features. B2B API: API Key-based usage plans — partner A gets 10,000 requests/day, partner B gets 100,000/day. CloudWatch metrics per API key show each partner's usage. Internal microservice: Lambda authoriser validates internal service token from Parameter Store — services authenticate with each other without shared passwords.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  Req[API Request + Bearer token] --> APIGW[API Gateway]
  APIGW --> Cache{Authoriser cache?}
  Cache -->|HIT: same token within 300s| Backend[Backend Lambda]
  Cache -->|MISS| AuthLambda[Authoriser Lambda]
  AuthLambda --> Valid{Token valid?}
  Valid -->|Yes| Policy[Return ALLOW + context]
  Valid -->|No| Deny[Raise Unauthorized → 401]
  Policy --> CacheStore[Cache policy 300s]
  CacheStore --> Backend
  Backend --> Resp[Response to client]

problems.title: API Gateway Timeout Debugging
problems.description: A Lambda function processes images and takes up to 45 seconds for large files. The API returns 504 Gateway Timeout after 29 seconds. The function itself completes successfully (you can see the result in S3). Propose two different solutions that handle this architectural constraint.
constraints: API Gateway hard limit: 29 seconds (cannot be changed)
Lambda max timeout: 15 minutes
Clients need to know when processing is complete

examples.output: Solution 1: Async pattern — POST returns 202 + jobId immediately, client polls GET /jobs/{jobId} for status. Solution 2: WebSocket API — client connects, server pushes completion event when done
examples.explanation: The 29-second API Gateway limit is architectural — the solution is to not wait synchronously for long operations

hints: 202 Accepted with a polling URL is the standard async REST pattern
POST /process-image → Lambda writes job to DynamoDB (status=PENDING) → invokes processing Lambda async → returns jobId
Alternatively: SQS + polling or SNS + WebSocket push

topics.title: Amazon DynamoDB — NoSQL at Scale
topics.description: DynamoDB is AWS's fully managed, serverless NoSQL database. It delivers single-digit millisecond latency at any scale, automatically scales capacity, and replicates data across 3 AZs. It's the database of choice for serverless architectures and high-throughput workloads.
topics.story: Imagine a massive library with millions of books. A traditional SQL database is like a librarian who must check every shelf systematically to find a book (full table scan) unless you use the catalog index. DynamoDB is like a library where every book has a unique tag, and there's a robot that can retrieve any book in under 10ms as long as you know the exact tag. Want to find books by a different attribute? Add a Global Secondary Index — it's like adding a second catalog sorted by author name instead of ISBN.
topics.analogy: Table = a single flat collection (no joins — each table is self-contained). Partition Key = the mandatory, unique first part of every item's address. Sort Key = optional second part — enables range queries within a partition. Item = a row (but schema-free — each item can have different attributes). Attribute = a column value (string, number, binary, list, map, set). GSI = a separate index with a different partition/sort key — enables queries on non-primary-key attributes. LSI = an index with the same partition key but different sort key — must be defined at table creation time.
topics.memoryAnchor: DynamoDB key rule: every query MUST specify the partition key. You can filter/sort by sort key. For any other attribute: use a GSI (extra cost, eventual consistency possible) or scan the whole table (expensive). Capacity modes: On-Demand (pay per request, no planning) vs Provisioned (set RCU/WCU, cheaper for predictable load). 1 RCU = 1 strongly consistent read of ≤4KB. 1 WCU = 1 write of ≤1KB.
topics.firstPrinciples: DynamoDB stores data in partitions (physical storage nodes). The partition key is hashed (Murmur3) to determine which physical partition holds the item — this is why all items with the same partition key land on the same node (enabling efficient range queries by sort key). A 'hot partition' (too many requests hitting one partition key) causes throttling — design partition keys for uniform distribution. The key design principle: know your access patterns BEFORE designing the table. Unlike SQL (normalise first, query later), DynamoDB requires you to model data around queries. Single-table design: store multiple entity types in one table, distinguished by a type prefix in the partition/sort key — eliminates cross-table joins (which DynamoDB doesn't support).
topics.bruteForce: Naive DynamoDB usage: one table per entity (Users, Orders, Products, Reviews), multiple GetItem calls to assemble a page. For a product detail page: 1 GetItem for product + N GetItems for reviews + 1 GetItem for seller. N+2 round trips. Correct approach: single-table design with access pattern modelling — one Query retrieves product + reviews + seller info in a single request.
topics.optimizedApproach: Single-table design: use composite sort keys like PRODUCT#{productId}, REVIEW#{timestamp}#{reviewId} — one Query with KeyConditionExpression='PK=PRODUCT#123 AND SK BETWEEN REVIEW# AND REVIEW$' fetches all reviews sorted by time. Use sparse indexes: put a GSI attribute only on items that need it — GSI size stays small. Use DynamoDB Streams + Lambda for event-driven architectures. Use TTL (Time To Live) for automatic expiry of session data, cache entries, temporary records — no delete cost. Use DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator) for microsecond reads if milliseconds aren't fast enough (caching layer in front of DynamoDB).
topics.whenToUse: DynamoDB: high-throughput key-value and document workloads, session stores, gaming leaderboards, IoT telemetry, shopping carts, user preferences, serverless backends. NOT DynamoDB: complex relational queries with multiple joins, ad-hoc analytics (use Athena/Redshift), full-text search (use OpenSearch), transactions spanning multiple tables with complex constraints (use RDS).

examples.title: Table Design, PutItem, GetItem, and Query
examples.code:
import boto3
from boto3.dynamodb.conditions import Key, Attr
from decimal import Decimal
import time

dynamodb = boto3.resource('dynamodb')
table = dynamodb.Table('UserOrders')

# ── Table design (created via CloudFormation or CDK) ──────────────────
# PK (Partition Key): USER#{userId}    → groups all user data together
# SK (Sort Key):      ORDER#{timestamp}#{orderId}  → time-sorted orders
# GSI: StatusIndex   → PK=status, SK=createdAt (find all PENDING orders)

# ── PutItem — write a new order ────────────────────────────────────────
def create_order(user_id, order_id, items, total):
    item = {
        'PK': f'USER#{user_id}',
        'SK': f'ORDER#{int(time.time() * 1000)}#{order_id}',
        'orderId': order_id,
        'userId': user_id,
        'status': 'PENDING',
        'items': items,  # List of dicts
        'total': Decimal(str(total)),  # DynamoDB uses Decimal for numbers
        'createdAt': int(time.time()),
        # TTL: auto-delete completed orders after 90 days
        'ttl': int(time.time()) + (90 * 24 * 3600)
    }
    table.put_item(
        Item=item,
        ConditionExpression='attribute_not_exists(PK)'  # Prevent overwrites
    )
    return order_id

# ── GetItem — O(1) exact lookup by PK + SK ────────────────────────────
def get_order(user_id, timestamp_ms, order_id):
    response = table.get_item(
        Key={
            'PK': f'USER#{user_id}',
            'SK': f'ORDER#{timestamp_ms}#{order_id}'
        },
        ConsistentRead=True  # Strong consistency (costs 2× RCUs vs eventual)
    )
    return response.get('Item')

# ── Query — fetch last 10 orders for a user ────────────────────────────
def get_user_orders(user_id, limit=10):
    response = table.query(
        KeyConditionExpression=Key('PK').eq(f'USER#{user_id}') &
                               Key('SK').begins_with('ORDER#'),
        ScanIndexForward=False,  # Sort descending (newest first)
        Limit=limit
    )
    return response['Items']

# ── Query with FilterExpression (post-query filter — NOT efficient) ────
def get_pending_orders_inefficient(user_id):
    # WARNING: FilterExpression runs AFTER DynamoDB fetches items from storage
    # Reads all user's orders, then filters — wastes RCUs on non-PENDING items
    response = table.query(
        KeyConditionExpression=Key('PK').eq(f'USER#{user_id}'),
        FilterExpression=Attr('status').eq('PENDING')
    )
    return response['Items']

# ── Query on GSI — find all PENDING orders (efficient) ────────────────
def get_all_pending_orders():
    response = table.query(
        IndexName='StatusIndex',
        KeyConditionExpression=Key('status').eq('PENDING'),
        ScanIndexForward=True  # Ascending by createdAt
    )
    return response['Items']

# ── UpdateItem — atomic update with condition ──────────────────────────
def confirm_order(user_id, sk):
    try:
        table.update_item(
            Key={'PK': f'USER#{user_id}', 'SK': sk},
            UpdateExpression='SET #s = :new_status, confirmedAt = :ts',
            ConditionExpression='#s = :expected_status',  # Optimistic lock
            ExpressionAttributeNames={'#s': 'status'},  # 'status' is reserved word
            ExpressionAttributeValues={
                ':new_status': 'CONFIRMED',
                ':expected_status': 'PENDING',
                ':ts': int(time.time())
            }
        )
    except dynamodb.meta.client.exceptions.ConditionalCheckFailedException:
        raise ValueError('Order is not in PENDING state — cannot confirm')
examples.explanation: DynamoDB's primary key determines query capability. GetItem requires the full PK+SK — it's an O(1) hash lookup, always fast. Query requires PK (exact match) and optionally SK (range/begins_with/between) — efficiently scans within a partition. Scan reads the entire table — avoid in production (expensive, slow). FilterExpression is a trap for beginners: it reduces the returned items but DynamoDB still reads and charges for ALL items scanned before filtering. Put your filter criteria in the key design instead. ExpressionAttributeNames is required for reserved words (status, name, count, etc.) — prefix them with # and declare the mapping.
examples.pseudocode:
DynamoDB query execution:
  Query: PK='USER#u42' AND SK begins_with 'ORDER#'
  
  1. Hash PK='USER#u42' → physical partition P3
  2. In P3, binary search for SK='ORDER#' (B-tree sorted by SK)
  3. Scan forward from that position, return items until:
     a. SK no longer begins with 'ORDER#', OR
     b. Limit reached, OR
     c. 1MB data read (pagination needed)
  
  FilterExpression (bad pattern):
    Steps 1-3 same as above (reads ALL ORDER# items)
    4. Apply filter: status='PENDING' — discard non-matching
    5. RCU charged for ALL items read in steps 1-3, not just returned items
    
  GSI query for status='PENDING':
    1. Hash 'PENDING' → GSI partition
    2. Return only PENDING items directly — no wasted reads
examples.realWorldUse: E-commerce order history: PK=USER#userId, SK=ORDER#timestamp#orderId. Gaming: PK=GAME#{gameId}, SK=SCORE#{score_padded}#{playerId} — Query returns top N scores for a game. IoT: PK=DEVICE#{deviceId}, SK=READING#{timestamp} — last 24h readings via Query with SK range. Session store: PK=SESSION#{token}, TTL attribute auto-expires sessions.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  PK[PK: USER#u42] -->|Hash| Part[Physical Partition P3]
  Part --> BTree[B-Tree sorted by SK]
  BTree --> Q1[Query: SK begins_with ORDER#]
  Q1 --> Items[Items: ORDER#1650000000#abc, ORDER#1650001000#def]
  GSI[GSI: StatusIndex] --> GPart[Partition: PENDING]
  GPart --> GItems[All PENDING orders across all users]
  PutItem -->|ConditionExpr: attribute_not_exists| Cond{PK exists?}
  Cond -->|No| Write[Write item]
  Cond -->|Yes| Fail[ConditionalCheckFailedException]

No → write succeeds"},{"step":3,"description":"get_user_orders('u42', limit=10) called","state":"Query: PK='USER#u42' AND SK begins_with 'ORDER#', ScanIndexForward=False"},{"step":4,"description":"DynamoDB hashes PK, locates partition P3, B-tree seeks to ORDER# prefix","state":"Returns 10 most recent orders (descending by SK = descending by timestamp)"},{"step":5,"description":"confirm_order('u42', 'ORDER#17...#O99') called","state":"UpdateItem: SET status='CONFIRMED' WHERE status='PENDING'"},{"step":6,"description":"Same confirm called twice (race condition test)","state":"Second call: ConditionExpression fails (status is now CONFIRMED, not PENDING) → ConditionalCheckFailedException"}]

examples.title: DynamoDB Streams and Transactions
examples.explanation: DynamoDB Streams captures every write (INSERT/MODIFY/REMOVE) as an ordered log, delivered to Lambda. This enables event-driven patterns without polling — react to data changes in real time. The stream record contains both the old and new image (if KEYS_AND_NEW_AND_OLD_IMAGES stream view type is selected), enabling 'what changed' logic. TTL deletions also appear in the stream with a special userIdentity field. Transactions (TransactWriteItems/TransactGetItems) provide ACID guarantees across multiple items/tables within the same AWS region — critical for financial operations, inventory management, and anywhere partial failure is unacceptable. Cost: 2× the normal WCU/RCU.
examples.pseudocode:
DynamoDB Streams delivery to Lambda:
  Table write occurs (PutItem/UpdateItem/DeleteItem)
  Shard router writes change record to stream shard
  Lambda event source mapping polls the shard:
    GetShards → GetShardIterator → GetRecords
  Records batched (up to batchSize) → Lambda invoked
  On success: checkpoint advanced (records won't be redelivered)
  On failure: retry (records redelivered, order preserved within shard)
  Records available in stream for 24 hours

Transaction internals (optimistic locking):
  1. Read current values of all items (transactGet phase)
  2. Validate all conditions
  3. If any condition fails: rollback all, return TransactionCanceledException
  4. If all conditions pass: write all items atomically
examples.realWorldUse: E-commerce: DynamoDB Stream triggers inventory update + warehouse notification + customer email on order status change. Credit systems: TransactWriteItems ensures debit + credit happen atomically — no double-spend. Search index sync: stream triggers OpenSearch document update when DynamoDB item changes. Audit log: stream captures all changes to a compliance log in S3.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  Write[UpdateItem: status=CONFIRMED] --> DDB[(DynamoDB Table)]
  DDB -->|Stream MODIFY record| Stream[DynamoDB Stream Shard]
  Stream -->|Lambda polls| SL[stream_handler Lambda]
  SL --> Email[send_confirmation_email]
  SL --> Inv[update_inventory]
  TX[transact_write_items] --> DDB
  DDB --> Cond{credits >= amount?}
  Cond -->|Yes| Commit[Atomic commit: debit + credit]
  Cond -->|No| Cancel[TransactionCanceledException]

problems.title: Design DynamoDB Table for a Messaging App
problems.description: Design a DynamoDB table for a messaging app. Access patterns needed: (1) Get all messages in a conversation, newest first. (2) Get a specific message by ID. (3) Get all conversations for a user, ordered by most recent message. (4) Find unread messages for a user. Define the PK, SK, and any GSIs needed.
constraints: Single-table design preferred
Conversation can have many messages
A user can be in many conversations

examples.input: 4 access patterns: fetch conversation messages, single message, user conversations, unread count
examples.output: PK=CONV#{convId} SK=MSG#{timestamp}#{msgId} for messages | PK=USER#{userId} SK=CONV#{lastMsgTs} for user conversations | GSI: PK=USER#{userId} SK=UNREAD#{convId} (sparse — only unread)
examples.explanation: Sparse GSI: only add the GSI key attributes to items where needed (unread messages) — keeps GSI small and cheap

hints: Use epoch milliseconds in sort key for time-ordered queries
User→Conversation mapping is a separate item type in the same table
Sparse GSI: only items with the GSI PK attribute appear in the index
problems.sampleInput: 4 access patterns: fetch conversation messages, single message, user conversations, unread count
problems.sampleOutput: PK=CONV#{convId} SK=MSG#{timestamp}#{msgId} for messages | PK=USER#{userId} SK=CONV#{lastMsgTs} for user conversations | GSI: PK=USER#{userId} SK=UNREAD#{convId} (sparse — only unread)

problems.title: DynamoDB Capacity Planning
problems.description: An e-commerce site has 100,000 daily active users. Each user loads their cart once per session (1 read, ~2KB item). Each user places 0.1 orders per day on average (1 write, ~1KB). Peak traffic is 5× average, occurring 2 hours per day. Calculate required RCUs and WCUs for both On-Demand and Provisioned capacity modes.
constraints: 1 RCU = 1 strongly consistent read of ≤4KB (or 2 eventually consistent reads)
1 WCU = 1 write of ≤1KB per second
Provisioned capacity: you set a fixed number, charged 24/7
On-Demand: charged per request, ~7× more expensive per operation

examples.input: 100k DAU, cart reads ~2KB, order writes ~1KB, peak 5× for 2h

problems.title: Fix a Hot Partition Problem
problems.description: A social media app stores posts with PK='POSTS' (all posts in one partition) and SK=postId. DynamoDB is throttling with ProvisionedThroughputExceededException during peak hours despite having 1000 WCU provisioned. Diagnose the problem and propose a fix.
constraints: All writes going to PK='POSTS' create a hot partition
Users query posts by tag and by date range
Write throughput needed: 200 writes/second

examples.input: PK='POSTS' for all posts — 200 writes/second all to one partition
examples.output: Problem: DynamoDB partition max is 1000 WCU, but a SINGLE partition key maxes at 1000 WCU — you're OK at 200 WPS. Real fix: distribute by PK=POST#{shardNumber} where shardNumber=hash(postId)%10, query with parallel Scan/BatchGetItem across 10 shards
examples.explanation: Actually 200 WPS on one partition key is within limits — but using PK='POSTS' for all posts still forces all reads/writes through one partition. Proper fix: PK=POST#{postId} — each post is its own partition, perfectly distributed

hints: Each unique PK value gets its own partition — distribution is automatic if PKs are diverse
Using a constant PK='POSTS' means everything goes to ONE partition (maximum ~1000 WCU)
Fix: use the actual post ID as the partition key — millions of unique posts = millions of partitions = automatic distribution

problems.sampleOutput: Problem: DynamoDB partition max is 1000 WCU, but a SINGLE partition key maxes at 1000 WCU — you're OK at 200 WPS. Real fix: distribute by PK=POST#{shardNumber} where shardNumber=hash(postId)%10, query with parallel Scan/BatchGetItem across 10 shards

topics.title: Amazon SQS — Decoupled Messaging
topics.description: SQS (Simple Queue Service) is a fully managed message queuing service that decouples producers from consumers. It buffers messages when consumers are slower than producers, enables retry logic, and guarantees at-least-once delivery — the backbone of resilient, async AWS architectures.
topics.story: Think of SQS as a post office system. The sender (producer) drops letters (messages) into the post box (SQS queue) and walks away — they don't wait for the recipient to open their door. The post office (SQS) holds the letters safely, retrying delivery if the recipient is busy. If a letter keeps failing to deliver (malformed, recipient code is broken), it goes to the dead letter shelf (DLQ) where a postmaster can inspect it. The sender and recipient never need to be online simultaneously.
topics.analogy: Standard Queue = postal system (at-least-once delivery, best-effort ordering, massive throughput). FIFO Queue = registered mail service (exactly-once delivery, guaranteed order, 3000 msg/s). Message = the letter/payload (up to 256KB). Visibility Timeout = the time the postman holds the letter after attempting delivery (preventing other postmen from trying while this one is working). Dead Letter Queue = the undeliverable letter pile after N failed attempts. Long Polling = staying at the post box for 20 seconds waiting for letters (vs short polling = checking once and leaving).
topics.memoryAnchor: SQS key numbers: max message size = 256KB (use S3 for larger). Default visibility timeout = 30s. Max visibility timeout = 12h. Message retention = 4 days (default) to 14 days max. Long polling wait time = 20s max. FIFO: 3000 msg/s with batching, 300 msg/s without. Standard: virtually unlimited throughput. At-least-once delivery = you MUST make your consumer idempotent.
topics.firstPrinciples: SQS uses a distributed queue architecture — messages are stored redundantly across multiple servers. When a consumer calls ReceiveMessage, SQS marks the message as 'in flight' (invisible to other consumers) for the visibility timeout duration. If the consumer acknowledges (DeleteMessage) before the timeout, the message is gone. If the consumer crashes or times out, the visibility timeout expires and the message becomes visible again for redelivery — this is the at-least-once guarantee. Consequence: your consumer MUST be idempotent (processing the same message twice should produce the same result as processing it once). FIFO queues add a deduplication layer (content-based or message deduplication ID) to prevent duplicate processing.
topics.bruteForce: Direct synchronous coupling: Order Service calls Inventory Service directly via HTTP. If Inventory Service is down: order fails. If Inventory Service is slow: order request times out. If order traffic spikes 10×: Inventory Service is overwhelmed. With SQS: Order Service writes to queue and returns success immediately. Inventory Service processes at its own rate. Inventory Service crashes: messages wait in queue and are retried automatically. Traffic spike: messages queue up, Inventory Service processes them over time.
topics.optimizedApproach: Use Long Polling (WaitTimeSeconds=20) — reduces empty ReceiveMessage responses by 99%, cutting costs significantly. Set visibility timeout to 6× Lambda timeout (prevent double-processing). Use DLQ with CloudWatch alarm on DLQ message count — alerts when processing is broken. Use message batching (send up to 10 messages in one API call) to reduce cost. For ordering-critical workloads: FIFO queue with MessageGroupId for per-group ordering. Use SQS Extended Client Library (Java) or S3 + pointer pattern for messages > 256KB.
topics.whenToUse: SQS: decoupling services, async job processing, handling traffic spikes, order processing queues, notification pipelines, any workflow where producer/consumer should operate independently. NOT SQS: real-time messaging between users (use WebSocket/AppSync), need for millions of subscribers (use SNS), message replay after consumer failure (use Kinesis — messages are retained and replayable), pub/sub fan-out to multiple consumers (use SNS → multiple SQS queues).

examples.title: Producer, Consumer, and Dead Letter Queue
examples.code:
import boto3
import json
import hashlib
import time

sqs = boto3.client('sqs')

QUEUE_URL = 'https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789012/order-processing'
DLQ_URL   = 'https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789012/order-processing-dlq'

# ── PRODUCER — order service sends a message ──────────────────────────
def place_order(order_id, user_id, items, total):
    message = {
        'orderId': order_id,
        'userId': user_id,
        'items': items,
        'total': total,
        'timestamp': int(time.time())
    }
    
    response = sqs.send_message(
        QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
        MessageBody=json.dumps(message),
        # MessageDeduplicationId for FIFO queues (prevents duplicates)
        # MessageGroupId for FIFO queues (orders per-group processing)
        MessageAttributes={
            'OrderType': {
                'DataType': 'String',
                'StringValue': 'STANDARD'
            }
        },
        # Delay delivery by 5 seconds (useful for retry backoff patterns)
        DelaySeconds=0
    )
    
    print(f"Message sent: {response['MessageId']}")
    return response['MessageId']

# ── BATCH PRODUCER — send up to 10 messages at once (cheaper) ─────────
def place_orders_batch(orders):
    entries = [
        {
            'Id': str(i),  # Client-side deduplication within batch
            'MessageBody': json.dumps(order)
        }
        for i, order in enumerate(orders[:10])  # Max 10 per batch
    ]
    
    response = sqs.send_message_batch(
        QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
        Entries=entries
    )
    
    if response.get('Failed'):
        for failure in response['Failed']:
            print(f"Failed to send: {failure['Id']} — {failure['Message']}")

# ── CONSUMER — manual polling (vs Lambda trigger) ─────────────────────
def process_messages():
    while True:
        response = sqs.receive_message(
            QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
            MaxNumberOfMessages=10,        # Retrieve up to 10
            WaitTimeSeconds=20,            # Long polling — wait up to 20s
            VisibilityTimeout=60,          # Hide from other consumers for 60s
            MessageAttributeNames=['All']
        )
        
        messages = response.get('Messages', [])
        if not messages:
            continue  # Long polling returned empty — loop again
        
        for msg in messages:
            receipt_handle = msg['ReceiptHandle']  # Needed for deletion
            body = json.loads(msg['Body'])
            
            try:
                process_order(body)
                
                # SUCCESS: delete from queue (acknowledge)
                sqs.delete_message(
                    QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
                    ReceiptHandle=receipt_handle
                )
                print(f"Processed and deleted: {body['orderId']}")
            
            except Exception as e:
                print(f"Failed to process {body['orderId']}: {e}")
                # Do NOT delete — message becomes visible again after VisibilityTimeout
                # After maxReceiveCount (e.g., 3) failed attempts → SQS moves to DLQ

def process_order(order):
    # MUST BE IDEMPOTENT — same message may be delivered twice
    order_id = order['orderId']
    
    # Idempotency check: if order already exists in DB, skip
    if order_already_processed(order_id):
        print(f"Order {order_id} already processed — skipping (idempotency)")
        return
    
    # Process the order...
    print(f"Processing new order {order_id}")
    # save_order_to_db(order)
    # charge_payment(order)
    # update_inventory(order['items'])

def order_already_processed(order_id):
    # Check DynamoDB or Redis for existence
    return False  # Placeholder

# ── DLQ MONITOR — process failed messages manually ────────────────────
def inspect_dlq():
    response = sqs.receive_message(
        QueueUrl=DLQ_URL,
        MaxNumberOfMessages=10,
        WaitTimeSeconds=5
    )
    for msg in response.get('Messages', []):
        body = json.loads(msg['Body'])
        print(f"DLQ message: orderId={body.get('orderId')} — investigate")
        # Could: alert on-call, write to incident tracker, attempt manual fix
examples.explanation: SQS message flow: producer sends → message stored in SQS → consumer calls ReceiveMessage (message becomes invisible) → consumer processes → consumer calls DeleteMessage (message permanently deleted). If consumer fails to delete within VisibilityTimeout, the message reappears. After maxReceiveCount failed attempts (tracked by SQS's ApproximateReceiveCount attribute), the message is moved to the DLQ. Long polling (WaitTimeSeconds=20) waits up to 20s for a message to arrive — dramatically reduces API calls and cost vs short polling (returns immediately even if queue is empty). Batch send/receive reduces API calls by up to 10×.
examples.pseudocode:
SQS visibility timeout state machine:
  Message state: AVAILABLE → IN_FLIGHT → AVAILABLE (or DELETED or DLQ)
  
  1. ReceiveMessage:
     Message: AVAILABLE → IN_FLIGHT (invisible, locked for VisibilityTimeout=60s)
  2a. Consumer succeeds:
     DeleteMessage → DELETED (gone permanently)
  2b. Consumer crashes/timeout:
     60s passes → IN_FLIGHT → AVAILABLE (ApproximateReceiveCount++)
  2c. ApproximateReceiveCount == maxReceiveCount (e.g., 3):
     SQS moves message to DLQ automatically

Idempotency pattern:
  Message received → extract idempotency key (orderId)
  Check key in idempotency store (DynamoDB/Redis):
    EXISTS: message already processed → skip, delete from queue
    NOT EXISTS: process message → store key → delete from queue
examples.realWorldUse: Order processing pipeline: payment service sends to SQS after charge, fulfillment service consumes and picks items, shipping service consumes and creates labels — all decoupled. Email notification system: user action triggers SQS message, email Lambda consumes at its own rate (no email throttling). Video transcoding queue: upload triggers SQS, transcoding Lambda consumes (heavyweight, takes minutes) — Lambda scales independently from uploader. Webhook retry: failed webhook deliveries go to SQS with exponential backoff via DelaySeconds.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  Producer[Order Service] -->|send_message| Queue[(SQS Queue)]
  Queue -->|ReceiveMessage: IN_FLIGHT| Consumer[Consumer Lambda]
  Consumer -->|success| Delete[DeleteMessage: GONE]
  Consumer -->|failure / crash| Timeout[VisibilityTimeout expires]
  Timeout --> Queue
  Queue -->|after 3 retries| DLQ[(Dead Letter Queue)]
  DLQ --> Alert[CloudWatch Alarm]
  Alert --> Oncall[PagerDuty alert]

examples.title: FIFO Queues and SNS Fan-Out Pattern
examples.code:
import boto3
import json
import hashlib

sqs = boto3.client('sqs')
sns = boto3.client('sns')

# ── FIFO Queue — ordered, exactly-once processing ─────────────────────
# FIFO queue URL must end with .fifo
FIFO_URL = 'https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789012/payments.fifo'

def send_payment_fifo(payment_id, user_id, amount, idempotency_key=None):
    """
    FIFO queue guarantees:
    1. Messages within same MessageGroupId are processed in order
    2. Exactly-once delivery (within 5-minute deduplication window)
    """
    message = {
        'paymentId': payment_id,
        'userId': user_id,
        'amount': amount
    }
    
    # MessageDeduplicationId: SQS rejects duplicate within 5-minute window
    # If not provided, must enable content-based deduplication on queue
    dedup_id = idempotency_key or hashlib.md5(
        f"{payment_id}-{user_id}-{amount}".encode()
    ).hexdigest()
    
    response = sqs.send_message(
        QueueUrl=FIFO_URL,
        MessageBody=json.dumps(message),
        MessageGroupId=f'USER-{user_id}',   # Orders payments PER USER
        MessageDeduplicationId=dedup_id      # Dedup within 5-min window
    )
    return response['MessageId']

# ── SNS Fan-Out — one message, multiple subscribers ────────────────────
# Use case: 'order placed' event → notify inventory + email + analytics
TOPIC_ARN = 'arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:order-events'

def publish_order_event(order_id, user_id, event_type, payload):
    """SNS topic → delivers to all subscribers (SQS queues, Lambda, email)"""
    message = {
        'eventType': event_type,  # 'ORDER_PLACED', 'ORDER_CANCELLED', etc.
        'orderId': order_id,
        'userId': user_id,
        'payload': payload
    }
    
    response = sns.publish(
        TopicArn=TOPIC_ARN,
        Message=json.dumps(message),
        Subject=f'Order Event: {event_type}',
        # MessageAttributes for SNS filter policies
        MessageAttributes={
            'eventType': {
                'DataType': 'String',
                'StringValue': event_type
            }
        }
    )
    return response['MessageId']

# ── SNS Filter Policy — subscribers receive only relevant events ───────
# Configured on the SQS subscription, not in code:
# Inventory queue filter: {"eventType": ["ORDER_PLACED", "ORDER_CANCELLED"]}
# Analytics queue filter: {"eventType": ["ORDER_PLACED", "ORDER_SHIPPED", "ORDER_CANCELLED"]}
# Email queue filter:     {"eventType": ["ORDER_CONFIRMED", "ORDER_SHIPPED"]}
#
# When ORDER_PLACED is published:
#   Inventory SQS: receives it (matches filter)
#   Analytics SQS: receives it (matches filter)
#   Email SQS:     does NOT receive it (no match)

# ── Consumer with SQS Extended Client (large messages) ────────────────
def send_large_payload(payload_dict, large_data_bytes):
    """
    SQS max = 256KB. For larger payloads:
    1. Upload actual data to S3
    2. Send SQS message with S3 pointer
    """
    s3 = boto3.client('s3')
    s3_key = f'sqs-payloads/{payload_dict["orderId"]}.bin'
    
    s3.put_object(
        Bucket='message-payloads',
        Key=s3_key,
        Body=large_data_bytes
    )
    
    message = {
        **payload_dict,
        's3Pointer': {'bucket': 'message-payloads', 'key': s3_key}
    }
    sqs.send_message(QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL, MessageBody=json.dumps(message))
    # Consumer fetches s3Pointer, downloads from S3, deletes S3 object after processing
examples.explanation: FIFO queues solve ordering and deduplication but have lower throughput (3,000 msg/s with batching) and higher cost than Standard. MessageGroupId acts like a partition key — all messages with the same group ID are processed in order. Different group IDs can be processed in parallel. This enables per-user ordering (payments for user A are ordered; payments for user A and user B processed concurrently). SNS fan-out is the standard pattern for 1-to-many event distribution. SNS delivers to subscribers (SQS queues, Lambda, HTTP endpoints, email) simultaneously. Filter policies on SQS subscriptions ensure each subscriber only receives relevant event types — eliminates unnecessary Lambda invocations and reduces processing costs.
examples.pseudocode:
SNS fan-out topology:
  SNS Topic: order-events
  |
  ├── SQS: inventory-queue    [filter: ORDER_PLACED, ORDER_CANCELLED]
  |    └── Lambda: inventory-service
  |
  ├── SQS: analytics-queue    [filter: ORDER_PLACED, ORDER_SHIPPED, ORDER_CANCELLED]
  |    └── Lambda: analytics-service
  |
  └── SQS: email-queue        [filter: ORDER_CONFIRMED, ORDER_SHIPPED]
       └── Lambda: email-service

  publish ORDER_PLACED:
    → inventory-queue: YES (matches)
    → analytics-queue: YES (matches)
    → email-queue: NO (filter mismatch)
    
  publish ORDER_CONFIRMED:
    → inventory-queue: NO
    → analytics-queue: NO
    → email-queue: YES
examples.realWorldUse: Payment processing: FIFO queue ensures refund is processed before the next charge for the same user (prevents incorrect balance). Order lifecycle events: SNS topic for all order events, each downstream service subscribes to only what it cares about. Video processing: upload event → SNS → 3 SQS queues → (1) transcoding Lambda, (2) thumbnail Lambda, (3) metadata extraction Lambda — all run in parallel from one S3 upload event.
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
  App[Application] -->|publish| SNS[SNS Topic: order-events]
  SNS -->|ORDER_PLACED filter| InvQ[(SQS: inventory-queue)]
  SNS -->|ORDER_PLACED filter| AnalQ[(SQS: analytics-queue)]
  SNS -->|ORDER_CONFIRMED filter| EmailQ[(SQS: email-queue)]
  InvQ -->|Lambda trigger| InvL[Inventory Lambda]
  AnalQ -->|Lambda trigger| AnalL[Analytics Lambda]
  EmailQ -->|Lambda trigger| EmailL[Email Lambda]
  FIFO[(SQS FIFO: payments.fifo)] -->|MessageGroupId=USER-u42| Ord[Ordered per user]
